claims.normalized_claim: Aaron Swartz (born November 8, 1986) was a programming prodigy who, as a teenager, co-authored the RSS 1.0 specification (published December 2000) as part of the RSS-DEV Working Group.
counterevidence: RSS has multiple lineages (RSS 0.9x and RSS 1.0); Swartz did not invent the concept of RSS.
allowed_wording: As a teenager, he co-authored the RSS 1.0 specification.
He helped write the technical standard that taught the web to syndicate itself.
claims.notes: Wording lock: co-authored RSS 1.0, NOT invented RSS.

claims.normalized_claim: Swartz was a key collaborator on Markdown, the lightweight text-to-HTML formatting language created by John Gruber, and wrote an early implementation in its lineage.
evidence_locations: Gruber's acknowledgements of Swartz's collaboration on Markdown
counterevidence: Markdown is credited to John Gruber as its creator; Swartz was a collaborator, not the creator.
allowed_wording: He was a key collaborator on Markdown.
He helped shape Markdown, the writing format much of the web still uses.
claims.notes: Wording lock: KEY COLLABORATOR on Markdown, NOT co-created.

claims.normalized_claim: As a teenager, Swartz helped build the technical layer of Creative Commons, working on the metadata that let open licenses be read by machines.
evidence_locations: Documentary record of his CC involvement
counterevidence: He did not found Creative Commons; he was a young technical contributor.
allowed_wording: He helped build the technical layer of Creative Commons.
He wrote code that let a license tell a machine 'you may share this.'
claims.notes: Wording lock: helped build the technical layer, not founded.

claims.normalized_claim: Swartz's startup Infogami merged with Reddit in 2006, making him a co-owner of the combined company; he is widely described as a Reddit co-founder, but that label is disputed because he joined through the merger rather than founding Reddit.
counterevidence: Reddit was founded by Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian; Swartz arrived via the Infogami merger.
allowed_wording: When his company merged with Reddit, he became a co-owner of the combined business.
He is often called a Reddit co-founder, though he joined through a merger, and the title is disputed.
claims.notes: Wording lock: co-owner via the Infogami merger; co-founder is DISPUTED. State both.

claims.normalized_claim: Swartz held a strong belief in open access to knowledge; in 2008 he wrote the 'Guerilla Open Access Manifesto,' arguing that locking up scholarly knowledge behind paywalls was unjust.
counterevidence: The manifesto's call to share paywalled material was used by prosecutors to suggest intent; its meaning is contested.
allowed_wording: He believed knowledge paid for by the public should belong to the public.
In 2008 he wrote a manifesto calling for open access to locked-up research.
prohibited_wording: The manifesto proves he intended to commit a crime.
He was a thief by his own admission.
claims.notes: Present the manifesto as belief/context; the prosecution's use of it is contested. Do not treat it as a confession.

claims.normalized_claim: Swartz founded the advocacy group Demand Progress and was a central organizer of the broad 2011-2012 online campaign that helped defeat the SOPA and PIPA anti-piracy bills, which were shelved after a mass internet protest on January 18, 2012.
counterevidence: The campaign was a broad coalition (Wikipedia, tech companies, millions of users); credit is shared, not solely his.
allowed_wording: He founded Demand Progress and was a central organizer of the campaign that helped defeat SOPA and PIPA.
He helped turn a quiet bill into a fight the public won.
claims.notes: Central organizer of a coalition; the campaign 'helped defeat' the bills. Share credit.

claims.normalized_claim: Between 2010 and 2011, using MIT's open campus network (to which he was not affiliated), Swartz downloaded roughly 4.8 million academic articles, about 80% of the JSTOR archive.
evidence_locations: ~4.8 million articles alleged in the indictment
MIT network; Swartz not an MIT student/employee
counterevidence: The number is from the prosecution; the figure of 'about 80%' is widely reported but should be framed as an estimate of scale.
allowed_wording: He downloaded about 4.8 million scholarly articles, most of the JSTOR archive, over MIT's open network.
He was not an MIT student or employee; the campus network was simply open.
prohibited_wording: He stole 4.8 million articles (as an established fact rather than the charge).
claims.notes: Frame the scale as alleged/estimated; avoid 'hacked' and 'stole' as narrator fact.

claims.normalized_claim: JSTOR, the party whose articles were downloaded, reached a civil settlement with Swartz, secured its content, and stated it did not seek or want criminal charges; MIT, which owned the network, took a position of neutrality.
evidence_locations: JSTOR statement: civil settlement; no criminal prosecution sought
allowed_wording: JSTOR settled with him and said plainly it did not want him prosecuted.
MIT, whose network it was, chose to stay neutral.
claims.notes: Core irony: the 'victim' did not want charges. Attribute to JSTOR/MIT.

claims.normalized_claim: Federal prosecutors charged Swartz under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act and the wire fraud statute; the original July 2011 indictment had four counts, and a September 2012 superseding indictment expanded it to thirteen felony counts.
allowed_wording: He was charged under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act and for wire fraud.
Four felony counts in 2011 became thirteen in a 2012 superseding indictment.
claims.notes: He was never tried or convicted. State only charges. 4 -> 13 counts.

claims.normalized_claim: Announcing the case, U.S. Attorney Carmen Ortiz said, in substance, 'Stealing is stealing whether you use a computer command or a crowbar, and whether you take documents, data or dollars.'
counterevidence: Critics argued the framing equated open-network downloading with physical burglary.
allowed_wording: The U.S. Attorney, Carmen Ortiz, put it this way: 'Stealing is stealing whether you use a computer command or a crowbar.'
claims.notes: Attribute the quote to Ortiz as the prosecution's position; verify exact wording before publish.

claims.normalized_claim: The combined charges carried a theoretical maximum of about 35 years in prison, but that figure is the stacked statutory maximum; prosecutors signaled a plea of roughly six months (per his attorney, Elliot Peters), and legal scholars such as Orin Kerr argued the headline maximum wildly overstated his realistic sentencing exposure.
evidence_locations: Kerr: realistic exposure far below the maximum
counterevidence: Even a short sentence plus a felony record on his own terms was, to Swartz, unacceptable; the maximum was nonetheless real on paper.
allowed_wording: On paper the charges added up to as much as 35 years, the theoretical maximum if every count were stacked.
Prosecutors signaled they wanted a guilty plea and roughly six months, his attorney said.
Legal scholars argued the 35-year headline wildly overstated what he actually faced.
prohibited_wording: He was facing 35 years in prison.
He was going to spend 35 years behind bars.
The 35-year figure (stated alone, without the theoretical-maximum and realistic-plea context).
claims.notes: THE 35 YEARS LOCK: never unqualified. Always pair the theoretical stacked maximum with the ~6-month plea signal and the scholarly critique.

claims.normalized_claim: Swartz declined to accept a plea deal that required him to plead guilty to felony charges, choosing instead to fight the case.
evidence_locations: Account of plea negotiations; his refusal of a felony plea
allowed_wording: He would not plead guilty to being a felon, so the case kept moving toward trial.
prohibited_wording: He admitted he was a criminal.
claims.notes: His refusal of a felony plea; attribute to the record/attorney accounts.

claims.normalized_claim: Aaron Swartz died by suicide on January 11, 2013, at age 26.
allowed_wording: Aaron Swartz died by suicide on January 11, 2013, at age 26.
prohibited_wording: any reenactment or scene of the death
claims.notes: SAFE-HANDLING: state ONCE, exactly this wording. No method, location, note, scene, or reenactment. NEVER 'committed suicide.'

claims.normalized_claim: In an official statement, Swartz's family and partner said that decisions made by prosecutors and by MIT contributed to his death, called it the product of a justice system rife with intimidation and prosecutorial overreach, and described the case as 'a crime with no victims'; they said MIT 'refused to stand up' for him.
counterevidence: This is the family's grief-stricken view; suicide is rarely attributable to a single cause, and the narrator must not adopt it as established fact.
allowed_wording: His family said the prosecution's overreach contributed to his death, and that MIT refused to stand up for him.
They called it a crime with no victims.
prohibited_wording: The prosecution caused his death (as narrator fact).
Carmen Ortiz drove him to suicide (as narrator fact).
claims.notes: NO single-cause narrative as fact. Causation language is ALWAYS attributed to the family statement, never asserted by the narrator.

claims.normalized_claim: Legal scholar Lawrence Lessig, a friend and mentor of Swartz, publicly criticized the prosecution as disproportionate, framing the government's conduct in terms of a 'bully' and questioning whether its behavior was proportional.
counterevidence: Lessig was personally close to Swartz; his view is a named opinion, not a neutral finding.
allowed_wording: Lawrence Lessig, his friend and mentor, asked whether the government's behavior had been remotely proportional, and called the posture that of a bully.
prohibited_wording: Everyone agreed the prosecution was evil.
Lessig proved the prosecution was illegal.
claims.notes: Attribute to Lessig as named opinion.

claims.normalized_claim: MIT commissioned an internal review (the 2013 Abelson Report) that found no MIT wrongdoing but criticized the university's hands-off, neutral posture and concluded MIT had 'missed an opportunity' to lead; Swartz's father publicly rejected the report's 'neutral' characterization.
allowed_wording: MIT's own review found no wrongdoing, but said the university had missed an opportunity to lead, and faulted its choice to stay neutral.
His father rejected the word 'neutral.'
prohibited_wording: The report proved MIT was guilty.
claims.notes: Attribute findings to the report; the rejection of 'neutral' to his father. Foreground systemic critique.

claims.normalized_claim: After his death, a CFAA reform bill known as 'Aaron's Law,' sponsored by Rep. Zoe Lofgren and Sen. Ron Wyden, was proposed but never passed; the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act was not meaningfully reformed.
evidence_locations: Aaron's Law introduced 2013; did not become law
allowed_wording: A bill to narrow the law, called Aaron's Law, was introduced, and it never passed.
The Computer Fraud and Abuse Act was not meaningfully changed.
prohibited_wording: The law was fixed in his name.
claims.notes: Confirm latest reintroduction status before publish; the lock is: proposed, never passed.

claims.normalized_claim: Swartz built tools and projects aimed at making public information freely accessible, including work on the Open Library and the RECAP effort to free U.S. federal court records (PACER) from paywalls.
counterevidence: The 2008-09 PACER episode drew an FBI inquiry that was closed with no charges; mention only as context if used.
allowed_wording: He built tools to pull public records out from behind paywalls, like court filings the public already owned.
prohibited_wording: He was already a known criminal before JSTOR.
claims.notes: Use as the 'pattern of freeing public knowledge' context; the PACER FBI look closed without charges.

findings: R3 + SENSITIVE (death by suicide). Death stated ONCE per CLM-0013 exact wording; no method/location/note/scene; never 'committed suicide.'
NO single-cause narrative: causation re his death is ALWAYS attributed to the family statement (CLM-0014), never asserted by the narrator. Systemic critique foregrounded.
THE 35 YEARS (CLM-0011) never appears unqualified: always the theoretical stacked statutory maximum, paired with the ~6-month plea signal (Peters) and the scholarly critique (Kerr).

Wording locks held: co-authored RSS 1.0 (not invented); key collaborator on Markdown (not co-created); helped build CC's technical layer (not founded); Reddit co-owner via Infogami merger, co-founder disputed.
He was charged, never tried or convicted (CLM-0009). JSTOR settled civilly and did not want charges; MIT neutral (CLM-0008). Carmen Ortiz quote attributed (CLM-0010).
988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline shown on-screen and in description; CTA kept quiet/respectful. No real-person likeness in any visual.

All facts web-verified at authoring; durable source content_hash pending -> compute and re-verify at fact_recheck before publish; dedicated R3 legal/rights + safe-handling review required pre-publish.
